sections.heading: Symptoms
sections.content: Esophageal cancer typically presents with progressive dysphagia, initially to solids then liquids, often accompanied by weight loss and anorexia. Dysphagia occurs due to luminal obstruction from the tumor. Patients may report food sticking, odynophagia (painful swallowing), or a sensation of retrosternal pressure. Weight loss is multifactorial, resulting from decreased oral intake, increased metabolic demands, and malabsorption. Local invasion can cause chest or back pain, cough, hoarseness (recurrent laryngeal nerve involvement), or aspiration pneumonia. Advanced disease may present with symptoms of metastasis (e.g., bone pain, jaundice) or complications like esophageal fistula or hemorrhage.

symptoms.name: Dysphagia
symptoms.detail: Progressive difficulty swallowing, typically starting with solids and advancing to liquids.

symptoms.name: Weight loss
symptoms.detail: Unintentional weight loss, often >10% of body weight.

symptoms.name: Chest/Back pain
symptoms.detail: Retrosternal or epigastric pain, may radiate to the back.

symptoms.name: Cough/Aspiration
symptoms.detail: Chronic cough, hoarseness, or recurrent pneumonia due to aspiration.

symptoms.name: Gastrointestinal bleeding
symptoms.detail: Hematemesis, melena, or iron deficiency anemia from tumor ulceration.

alarms.name: Red Flag
alarms.detail: Progressive dysphagia is a critical alarm feature.

alarms.name: Red Flag
alarms.detail: Significant weight loss is a concerning symptom.

alarms.name: Red Flag
alarms.detail: Persistent pain may indicate local invasion.

alarms.name: Red Flag
alarms.detail: Active bleeding is a critical alarm.
